author: Augustine of Hippo
book: Sermons on Selected Lessons of the New Testament.

classification.cautionReason: No major mainstream heresy warning is attached by the preview classifier.

lineage: Sermons on Selected Lessons of the New Testament.
On the words of the Gospel, Matt. xix. 17, ‘If thou wouldest enter into life, keep the commandments.’

ccel.title: NPNF1-06. St. Augustine: Sermon on the Mount; Harmony of the Gospels; Homilies on the Gospels

source.title: NPNF1-06. St. Augustine: Sermon on the Mount; Harmony of the Gospels; Homilies on the Gospels

title: On the words of the Gospel, Matt. xix. 17, ‘If thou wouldest enter into life, keep the commandments.’

source.title: NPNF1-06. St. Augustine: Sermon on the Mount; Harmony of the Gospels; Homilies on the Gospels

verses.book: Sermons on Selected Lessons of the New Testament.
verses.text: The Lord said to a certain young man, “If thou wilt enter into life, keep the commandments.”

verses.book: Sermons on Selected Lessons of the New Testament.
verses.text: He did not say, “If thou wilt enter into life eternal,” but “If thou wilt enter into life;” laying down that as life, which is to be life eternal.

verses.book: Sermons on Selected Lessons of the New Testament.
verses.text: Let us first then set forth the value of the love of this life.

verses.book: Sermons on Selected Lessons of the New Testament.
verses.text: For even this present life, under whatever circumstances, is loved; and men fear and dread to end it of whatever kind it be; however full of trouble and misery.

verses.book: Sermons on Selected Lessons of the New Testament.
verses.text: Hence may we see, hence consider, how the life eternal should be loved; when this life so miserable, and which must sometime come to an end, is loved so much.

verses.book: Sermons on Selected Lessons of the New Testament.
verses.text: Consider, Brethren, how greatly should that life be loved, where thou wilt never end life.

verses.book: Sermons on Selected Lessons of the New Testament.
verses.text: Thou dost love, it seems, this present life, where thou dost labour so much, hastest to and fro, art busy, sufferest fatigue; yea scarcely to be enumerated are the necessities of this miserable life; sowing, ploughing, clearing the ground, sailing, grinding, cooking, weaving; and after all these things thou hast to end thy life.

verses.book: Sermons on Selected Lessons of the New Testament.
verses.text: See the evils thou dost suffer in this miserable life, which thou lovest; and dost thou think that thou shalt always live, and never die?

verses.book: Sermons on Selected Lessons of the New Testament.
verses.text: Temples, stones, marbles, joined so strongly together with iron and lead, fall into ruin for all their strength; and does a man suppose that he shall never die?

verses.book: Sermons on Selected Lessons of the New Testament.
verses.text: Learn then, Brethren, to seek for eternal life, where you will not endure all this, but will reign with God for ever.

verses.book: Sermons on Selected Lessons of the New Testament.
verses.text: “For he who wisheth life,” as the Prophet says, “loveth to see good days.”

verses.book: Sermons on Selected Lessons of the New Testament.
verses.text: For in evil days death is rather wished for than life.

verses.book: Sermons on Selected Lessons of the New Testament.
verses.text: Do we not hear and see men when they are involved in some tribulations and distresses, in law-suits or sicknesses and they see that they are in travail, do we not hear them saying nothing else but, “O God, send me death, hasten my days”?

verses.book: Sermons on Selected Lessons of the New Testament.
verses.text: Yet when sickness comes, they run about, and physicians are fetched, and money and rewards are promised.

verses.book: Sermons on Selected Lessons of the New Testament.
verses.text: Death himself says to thee, “Lo, here I am, whom but a little while ago thou wert asking of the Lord, why wouldest thou fly from me now?

verses.book: Sermons on Selected Lessons of the New Testament.
verses.text: I have found thee to be a self-deceiver, and a lover of this miserable life.”

verses.book: Sermons on Selected Lessons of the New Testament.
verses.text: But as concerning these days which we are passing now, the Apostle says, “Redeeming the time, because the days are evil.”

verses.book: Sermons on Selected Lessons of the New Testament.
verses.text: Are not these days indeed evil which we spend in this corruptible flesh, in or under so heavy a load of the corruptible body, amid so great temptations, amid so great difficulties, where there is but false pleasure, no security of joy, a tormenting fear, a greedy covetousness, a withering sadness?

verses.book: Sermons on Selected Lessons of the New Testament.
verses.text: Lo, what evil days! yet no one is willing to end these same evil days, and hence men earnestly pray God that they may live long.

verses.book: Sermons on Selected Lessons of the New Testament.
verses.text: Yet what is it to live long, but to be long tormented?

verses.book: Sermons on Selected Lessons of the New Testament.
verses.text: What is it to live long, but to add evil days to evil days?

verses.book: Sermons on Selected Lessons of the New Testament.
verses.text: When boys are growing up, it is as if days are being added to them; whereas they do not know that they are being diminished; and their very reckoning is false.

verses.book: Sermons on Selected Lessons of the New Testament.
verses.text: For as we grow in up, the number of our days rather diminishes than increases.

verses.book: Sermons on Selected Lessons of the New Testament.
verses.text: Appoint for any man at his birth, for instance, eighty years; every day he lives, he diminishes somewhat of that sum.

verses.book: Sermons on Selected Lessons of the New Testament.
verses.text: Yet silly men rejoice at the oft-recurring birthdays, both of themselves and their children.

verses.book: Sermons on Selected Lessons of the New Testament.
verses.text: O sensible man!

verses.book: Sermons on Selected Lessons of the New Testament.
verses.text: If the wine in thy bottle is diminished, thou art sad; days art thou losing, and art thou glad?

verses.book: Sermons on Selected Lessons of the New Testament.
verses.text: These days then are evil; and so much the more evil, in that they are loved.

verses.book: Sermons on Selected Lessons of the New Testament.
verses.text: This world is so alluring, that no one is willing to finish a life of sorrow.

verses.book: Sermons on Selected Lessons of the New Testament.
verses.text: For the true, the blessed life is this, when we shall rise again, and reign with Christ.

verses.book: Sermons on Selected Lessons of the New Testament.
verses.text: For the ungodly too shall rise again but to go into the fire.

verses.book: Sermons on Selected Lessons of the New Testament.
verses.text: Life then is there again, but that which is blessed.

verses.book: Sermons on Selected Lessons of the New Testament.
verses.text: And blessed life there can be none but that which is eternal, where are “good days;” and those not many days, but one day.

verses.book: Sermons on Selected Lessons of the New Testament.
verses.text: They are called “days” after the custom of this life.

verses.book: Sermons on Selected Lessons of the New Testament.
verses.text: That day knows no rising, it knows no setting.

verses.book: Sermons on Selected Lessons of the New Testament.
verses.text: To that day there succeeds no to-morrow; because no yesterday precedes it.

verses.book: Sermons on Selected Lessons of the New Testament.
verses.text: This day, or these days, and this life, this true life, have we in promise.

verses.book: Sermons on Selected Lessons of the New Testament.
verses.text: It is then the reward of a certain work.

verses.book: Sermons on Selected Lessons of the New Testament.
verses.text: So if we love the reward, let us not fail in the work; and so shall we reign with Christ for ever.
